mission.title: Install and Configure Eraser on Kubernetes
mission.description: Eraser is a tool designed to help Kubernetes administrators clean up non-running images from all nodes in a cluster, ensuring efficient resource usage and management. Installing Eraser allows you to automate the cleanup process and maintain a tidy environment.

steps.title: Add the Helm repository
steps.description:
First, add the Eraser Helm chart repository to your local Helm configuration. This allows you to install Eraser using Helm.
```bash
helm repo add eraser https://eraser-dev.github.io/eraser/charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Eraser using Helm
steps.description:
Now, install the Eraser Helm chart. This command will create the necessary namespace and deploy the application.
```bash
helm install eraser eraser/eraser --namespace eraser-system --create-namespace --version 1.4.1
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the Eraser pods are running successfully in the eraser-system namespace.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace eraser-system
```

steps.title: Check ImageJob CRDs
steps.description:
Verify that the Eraser Custom Resource Definitions were created successfully:
```bash
kubectl get crd | grep eraser.sh
```
You should see `imagejobs.eraser.sh` and `imagelists.eraser.sh` registered.

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
To uninstall Eraser, first remove the Helm release:
```bash
helm uninstall eraser --namespace eraser-system
```

uninstall.title: Delete the Custom Resource Definitions
uninstall.description:
Next, clean up the Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) associated with Eraser:
```bash
kubectl delete crd imagejobs.eraser.sh imagelists.eraser.sh
```

uninstall.title: Remove the namespace
uninstall.description:
Finally, delete the namespace created for Eraser to clean up any remaining resources:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace eraser-system
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's a good practice to backup the current state of your Eraser installation:
```bash
helm get values eraser --namespace eraser-system > eraser-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update the Helm repository
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository to ensure you have the latest charts:
```bash
helm repo update
```

upgrade.title: Upgrade Eraser
upgrade.description:
Now, upgrade your Eraser installation to the latest version:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade eraser eraser/eraser --namespace eraser-system
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check the status of the Eraser pods to ensure the upgrade was successful:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace eraser-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in Pending state
troubleshooting.description:
If Eraser pods are stuck in the Pending state, check for insufficient resources in your cluster:
```bash
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> --namespace eraser-system
``` 
Ensure your nodes have enough resources available.

troubleshooting.title: Eraser not cleaning images
troubleshooting.description:
If Eraser is not cleaning images as expected, check the logs for the collector pod:
```bash
kubectl logs <collector-pod-name> --namespace eraser-system
``` 
Look for any errors or misconfigurations in the logs.

troubleshooting.title: Health checks failing
troubleshooting.description:
If health checks are failing, verify the health probe configuration:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace eraser-system -o jsonpath='{.items[*].status.containerStatuses[*].ready}'
``` 
Ensure the health probe settings are correctly configured in the values.yaml.

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Eraser will result in the Eraser pods running in the 'eraser-system' namespace, with no errors in the pod logs. You should also see the Eraser components actively cleaning up non-running images as per your configuration.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add eraser https://eraser-dev.github.io/eraser/charts
helm repo update

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have Kubernetes cluster access and Helm installed.
